title_en: The First Pillar: Belief in Allah
content_en:
Al-Uthaymin devotes substantial attention to the first and most foundational pillar of iman — belief in Allah — because it is the root from which all other elements of faith grow. He structures his explanation around four interconnected dimensions that together constitute complete and correct belief in Allah: belief in His existence (wujud), His lordship (rububiyyah), His right to be worshipped alone (uluhiyyah), and His names and attributes (al-asma was-sifat).

On the question of Allah's existence, the Sheikh notes that the innate human nature (fitrah) already attests to a Creator. He points to the Quranic verse, 'Were they created from nothing, or are they themselves the creators?' (52:35), as a logical argument that the universe's existence demands a Creator Who exists independently, without beginning or end. He also cites the testimony of the created world itself: the perfect order of the cosmos, the precise laws of nature, and the existence of life all point to a Maker possessed of infinite knowledge and power.

Regarding rububiyyah — Lordship — al-Uthaymin explains that this encompasses affirming that Allah alone is the Creator, Sustainer, Sovereign, and Controller of all affairs in the universe. Nothing happens except by His will and power. He notes that even the polytheists of the Arabian Peninsula acknowledged this dimension of tawhid, as evidenced by Quranic verses such as 52:35 and 29:61. The error of the mushrikeen was not denying Allah's creative power but associating partners with Him in worship.

The most consequential dimension — tawhid al-uluhiyyah — is where al-Uthaymin places the greatest emphasis. This is the tawhid for which the prophets were sent and around which the message of 'La ilaha illAllah' revolves. It means that no being other than Allah deserves any act of worship: no prayer, no supplication, no sacrifice, no vow, no reliance (tawakkul) in its fullest sense, no love of the intensity due only to Allah. The Sheikh carefully distinguishes the love owed to Allah from the natural love one feels for family or community — the former is an act of worship; the latter is permissible and even encouraged.

Finally, al-Uthaymin addresses the names and attributes of Allah (tawhid al-asma was-sifat), which he defines as affirming for Allah all the names and attributes He has affirmed for Himself in the Quran and authentic Sunnah, without distortion (tahrif), negation (ta'til), asking 'how' (takyif), or likening them to creation (tamthil). He explains the Athari middle path between the Jahmiyyah who stripped Allah of attributes and the Mujassimah who likened Allah to created beings. The Quran itself provides the guiding principle: 'There is nothing like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, All-Seeing' (42:11).

The chapter ends with practical implications: a Muslim who correctly believes in Allah will direct all worship exclusively to Him, will trust His decrees, will find comfort in His names (such as al-Rahman and al-Wadud), and will have a living, aware relationship with the Creator — not merely an abstract theological acknowledgment.
